data.title: China's industrial profits fall in October after two months of growth
data.description:
China's industrial profits fell in October, after two months of growth, official data showed on Thursday, as businesses grappled with lacklustre domestic demand and an export downturn.

The drop could fuel calls for more government support measures to encourage domestic consumption and wean the manufacturing powerhouse off its reliance on exports as China confronts tariffs and growing trade barriers.

Industrial profits at China's industrial firms declined 5.5% year-on-year in October, data from the National Bureau of Statistics showed on Thursday, after jumping 21.6% in September and 20.4% in August.

The downturn highlights persistent domestic challenges facing the world's second-largest economy, even as U.S.-China trade tensions show signs of easing. Growth momentum has faltered sharply, with third-quarter economic expansion slowing to its weakest pace in a year.

Growth in industrial profits was 1.9% in the first 10 months versus a 3.2% rise in the January-September period.

Official data released earlier this month showed underwhelming growth in October retail sales despite an eight-day national holiday and the start of the month-long Singles' Day sales festival.

Producer deflation eased in October but prices remained in contraction compared with the same period last year, and factory output grew at the weakest annual pace since August 2024.

Chinese leaders have signalled a sharper shift towards supporting consumption over the next five years, but have yet to inject new large-scale stimulus to this end. Meanwhile, elevated unemployment rates for young people as well as a prolonged property market downturn continue to dampen sentiment.

State-owned firms saw profits unchanged in the first 10 months. Private-sector firms posted a 1.9% increase while foreign firms recorded a 3.5% rise, the data showed.

Industrial profit figures cover firms with annual revenue of at least 20 million yuan ($2.82 million) from their main operations.

($1 = 7.0822 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Nikkei rises more than 1% as tech stocks rally on Wall Street strength
data.description:
By Junko Fujita

Japan's Nikkei share average rose more than 1% on Thursday, extending gains to a third session, as technology heavyweights tracked their Wall Street peers higher.

The Nikkei climbed 1.2% to 50,144.19 by 0123 GMT, crossing the key 50,000 level for the first time since November 20. The broader Topix rose 0.51% to 3,372.44.

"Technology stocks lifted Wall Street overnight, and the Japanese market exactly mirrored it today," said Shuutarou Yasuda, a market analyst at Tokai Tokyo Intelligence Laboratory.

Wall Street extended its rally on Wednesday as revived tech strength and the increasing probability of a December interest rate cut put investors in a buying mood the day before the Thanksgiving holiday.

In Japan, technology investor SoftBank Group rose 4.2% in a second straight session of gains. The stock fell to a more than two-month low earlier this week and traded nearly 40% below its late October peak on Thursday.

"Investors continued to buy SoftBank Group shares on a dip," Yasuda said.

Other artificial intelligence-related shares also rose, with chip-testing equipment maker Advantest jumping 4.45%, while chip-making equipment maker Tokyo Electron climbed 2.6%.

These three stocks together added about 440 points to the Nikkei, which rose by 620 points on Thursday.

Banks rose on growing bets of a Bank of Japan interest rate hike next month. Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group advanced 2% and Mizuho Financial Group gained 0.26%.

Swap rates indicated a 33.9% chance of a rate hike to 0.75% on December 19, when the Bank of Japan concludes its two-day policy meeting.

"In the previous meetings, two board members dissented to the hold decision and instead proposed raising rates, but looking at the recent BOJ communication, there could be at least four members who may support a rate hike," said Yasuda.

Of the more than 1,600 stocks trading on the Tokyo Stock Exchange's prime market, 69% rose, 25% fell and 4% traded flat.

data.title: 53 Hours and No Wi-Fi: Why I Loved Amtrak's Slow Train to San Francisco — WSJ
data.description:
By Dawn Gilbertson

The friendly competition began during happy hour Sunday, minutes after the Amtrak train pulled out of Chicago's Union Station.

"How far are you going?" passengers asked each other over and over in the Superliner's observation lounge.

Riding Amtrak's California Zephyr route, it turns out, is equal parts sightseeing extravaganza and endurance test.

I was in for the duration: 53 hours and 33 stops across seven states, aka the longer, pricier way from Chicago to San Francisco. And I wasn't the only die-hard (masochist?) who flew to Chicago to catch the train many describe as Amtrak's most scenic. One family flew in from Seattle to celebrate their train-loving son's 11th birthday, another from Atlanta and North Carolina for pre-Thanksgiving marathon sessions of Mexican Train Dominoes.

It sounds torturous on paper and in some Reddit reviews, but it turned out to be an oddly peaceful way to brave, and dare I say savor, holiday travel. All this despite no Wi-Fi, my own shower or bathroom or a solid night's sleep.

Coach or sleeper?

Any long Amtrak journey (this was my first) starts with a big choice: coach or sleeper car? The last-minute price for my three-day, two-night trip was $433 in coach or $1,200 for a small private room called a roomette. The latter comes with three meals a day in the dining car. The bedrooms with private bathroom and shower were sold out but go for more than $2,500. A last-minute one-way flight from Chicago to San Francisco, by comparison, was about $500. (The price for the roomettes and bedrooms are for the room, which can hold more than one person.)

I've done plenty of less-than-luxurious things for this job ( sharing a bathroom with an Airbnb host and camping at the Nascar championships). But I wasn't going to spend that much time without a bed or some privacy. I'm a lousy sleeper and had a tight deadline looming at the end of the ride.

So I picked a roomette. It features two seats that face each other by day and slide into a bed at night. There is also a pulldown upper bunk if you have two passengers. A friend who geeks out on YouTube train videos volunteered to join me, but I was thankful I was on my own given the tight quarters.

Tight doesn't mean unbearable. Yes, I banged my head on the upper bunk more than a few times, the temperature in the room fluctuated despite my settings and light seeped in even with the curtains velcroed shut.

But I had enough room to change clothes and relax with great views when I wasn't in the observation lounge or dining car. I even had one lunch delivered there. The mattress, blanket and pillows were surprisingly cozy.

Rookie mistake number one, though: not bringing industrial strength ear plugs. My room was in the front of the train (car 520, room 20), and the honking was incessant at night.

I was up almost every hour the first night and a few times the second, even with ear plugs from one of those Delta Air Lines in-flight amenity kits and Benadryl. Not helping: The first night was rockier than my recent trans-Atlantic crossing on a cruise ship, something Amtrak employees blamed on the condition of the tracks from Nebraska into Colorado.

Sitting with strangers

The best thing about the journey — even better than the gobsmacking views through Colorado — was spending time with fellow passengers. There were more than 200 of us on the train at the peak.

Because no one was in a hurry, the encounters felt far less fleeting than you would find in an airport lounge or on a flight. Call it corny, but the conversations and games (Yahtzee!) will stay with me.

Train travel invites camaraderie. It started with the welcome happy hour in the observation lounge on Sunday afternoon led by an engaging flight attendant-turned-bartender. (Happy hour was a cash bar; if you are in the sleeper car like I was, you get one free alcoholic beverage with dinner, otherwise you pay.)

He gamely answered all questions about the best spots to sit and when. The scenery doesn't take center stage until the first full day when you traverse Colorado, at points feeling like you are floating down the Colorado River.

I met a 23-year-old DePaul graduate from Chicago who was headed to visit her parents in wine country for Thanksgiving. Her friends thought she was crazy for taking the train — in coach no less — but she was excited. It cost her less than $300 one way.

And then there were train enthusiasts like an 11-year-old passenger, who was wearing a homemade California Zephyr shirt, a blue Amtrak baseball hat and a lanyard with collectible pins. He'd already been on Amtrak's Coast Starlight and Empire Builder and picked California Zephyr for his birthday trip with his parents. He spewed Amtrak facts like he was the conductor. (Two Genesis locomotives powered the train, he told me.) I spent time with them every day.

Amtrak doesn't play favorites in the dining car, so meals are another built-in meetup. You make a reservation and are seated at the first open booth. For solo travelers or couples, that means plopping down next to strangers.

The dining scene

I spent a delightful dinner the first night with Roger Brown and two of his kids, ages 7 and 11. They started their Amtrak trek in Burlington, Vt., on Saturday, enjoying a holiday parade in Schenectady, N.Y., and renting e-bikes in Chicago before we all boarded the California Zephyr Sunday afternoon. Brown's wife and two other children were flying to Colorado to meet them for Thanksgiving and skiing.

He even shared some of his family's maple syrup to use on Amtrak's french toast at breakfast. Turns out he was named the 2014 Maple Person of the Year by the Vermont Maple Sugar Makers Association, an accolade that didn't come up at dinner.

At breakfast on Tuesday, I sat with a teacher from Grand Junction, Colo., and a mother-daughter duo from Salt Lake City. They were all seated in coach and paid $20 each for the breakfast quesadillas and omelets. (I like how Amtrak isn't elitist when it comes to access to the dining car and observation lounge.)

Speaking of the food, no one boards a train for three days for the food, at least in the United States. I had a good first-class meal on Amtrak from New York to Boston a few years ago, but didn't know what to expect on the California Zephyr.

The Orient Express it wasn't, but every meal hit. Favorites included the cooked-to-order Angus flatiron steak and mashed potatoes, breakfast potatoes and chicken Caesar salad. I thanked one of the chefs when I ran into him as the trip ended.

That doesn't mean I wasn't counting the minutes until I could order my favorite hotel lobster roll as the train traveled along the water at sunset on approach to Emeryville, Calif., our last stop.

The trip dispatches and countless photos and videos I sent my family — none a train enthusiast — inspired one of them to give it a try.

In a group chat about our annual gift exchange my youngest sister said: "Whoever has me, I want to go on Dawn's train."

Write to Dawn Gilbertson at dawn.gilbertson@wsj.com

data.title: US directs diplomats to lobby governments against mass migration, cable says
data.description:
Key points:
  • US diplomats ordered to raise concerns over migrant populations
  • Trump administration sees mass migration as linked to crime, rights abuses
  • Trump unveiled aggressive anti-immigration agenda

By Humeyra Pamuk

The Trump administration last week ordered U.S. diplomats abroad to lobby against pro-migration policies and raise concerns over what it says are migrant populations committing violent crimes, according to a State Department cable seen by Reuters.

The cable, sent on Friday to dozens of U.S. embassies across Europe, Canada and Australia, argues that crime and human rights abuses linked to mass migration and "individuals of a migration background" were a significant concern in Europe and the West. It says these incidents threaten public safety and social cohesion around the world.

It instructs U.S. missions to report to Washington on such crimes and abuses and to provide analysis of how the host country reacts, while pushing the governments to reform migration policies and limit any programs that enable mass migration.

"We encourage your government to ensure that policies protect your citizens from the negative social impacts of mass migration, including displacement, sexual assault, and the breakdown of law and order," reads one of more than a dozen talking points the State Department provided to U.S. diplomats in the cable, which was first reported by the New York Times.

Anti-immigration was a major part of Donald Trump's presidential campaign. After taking office, he launched an aggressive enforcement campaign, surging troops to the southern border and pledging to deport millions of immigrants who were in the U.S. illegally.

The Republican president has repeatedly blamed migrants in the U.S. illegally for fueling violent crime, although studies show immigrants are not more likely to commit crimes.

The administration has also worked to internationalize its restrictive approach. In September, top officials urged other nations to join a global campaign to roll back asylum protections, a major shift that would seek to reshape the post-World War Two framework around humanitarian migration.

Late last month Trump slashed the limit on refugee admissions for fiscal 2026 to a record low 7,500 from the 100,000 who entered under then-President Joe Biden in fiscal 2024. Trump said his administration would focus on bringing in white South Africans of Afrikaner ethnicity.

A State Department spokesperson, asked for comment on the cable, said mass migration was a human rights issue and that it regularly led to a rise in violent crimes. The spokesperson did not provide any data to support the assertion.

The cable, which quotes Trump as saying that "a nation without borders is not a nation," also asks governments to resist practices that "disproportionately favor migrant populations at the expense of local communities, including displacement, legal consequences for criticizing mass migration."

data.title: Japan Reflation Unlocks Investment Opportunity in Real Estate
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--Japan's economy is finally reflating after years of stagnation. Though rising interest rates are typically seen as a headwind for real estate, in Japan, the shift is unlocking opportunities for foreign investment, says LaSalle Investment Management's Brian Klinksiek.

Klinksiek, global head of research and strategy at the real-estate investment management firm, spoke with The Wall Street Journal about Japan's property landscape as interest rates climb, and markets are reshaped by post-pandemic macroeconomic shifts and geopolitics.

Edited excerpts follow.

Q: How would you describe Japan's property market now?

Klinksiek: Shifting to a more normal context of inflation has everyone focusing on the interest rates increasing. In isolation, you'd say interest rates increasing is a bad thing for real estate. But what's also happening is rents are increasing. There needs to be a shift in thinking on the way leases are written, the way assets are managed, the way the whole economy is managed, and the opportunity for foreign inbound investment into Japan. What we've seen so far is that this interest rate increase has largely been made up for by rental increases.

I think what the BOJ [Bank of Japan] is doing has scared away investors who are not clever enough to see how to drive value and attracted investors who see lots of examples to benefit from this generational transition.

Q: What is driving diversification among Japanese institutional investors in outbound investment?

Klinksiek: I would say global fracturing has created less correlation between parts of the globe. The change over the last year in Europe, particularly Germany, announcing that they were going to invest more and spend more in their economy...and that they were going to have to forge trading relationships more deeply globally in the face of the trade war was a reminder that the world is now more complex than it being a single dominant market.

Q: Where does Japan stand as the property investment destination compared to other Asia Pacific countries?

Klinksiek: Japan is at the absolute top of the list in the Asia-Pacific region because it's big, it's transparent, it has a good relationship with the U.S. geopolitically and also has the balancing factor of domestic capital being readily available.

The China market is quite challenged right now. Asset valuations and fundamentals will eventually recover but there won't be a lot of global capital participating in that. It'll be much more domestic capital because of the geopolitical considerations, the relationship with China and the West--the U.S. especially.

Q: Are global real-estate investors reallocating from China to Japan?

Klinksiek: The short answer is yes. The form that generally takes is that Pan-Asia-Pacific funds have moved their allocations up for Japan and down for China, and in some cases down to zero for China. I don't expect global capital allocations to China to increase anytime soon.

I think Japan will have several years of strength ahead of it, coming from the opportunity to participate in this transition from the old no-inflation model to the new operating model.

Q. What are the major shifts we're seeing in global real-estate markets right now?

Klinksiek: After the disruption from Covid and the repricing that happened with the great tightening cycle with rates increasing, we were declaring that we saw a positive trajectory again for real-estate values after a period of challenges. That recovery was only partial in 2025. One of the big reasons for that was the volatility that we saw coming from global politics and geopolitics.

Are we stuck in a "Groundhog Day" loop? We actually argue that we're not likely to be stuck in that loop--we are going to break out of that loop because there are three conditions in place which are positive for the direction of global real-estate markets.

The first one is that the prices are now correctly aligned with interest rates. The repricing of global real estate is largely complete. Two is that we see a positive setup for real-estate fundamentals. The third condition that needs to be in place is for debt to be readily available. We've seen a huge healing in real-estate debt markets.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Gold Hovers Near Two-Week High
data.description:
Gold prices dipped to around $4,150 per ounce on Thursday but remained near a two-week high, as investors continued to anticipate a Federal Reserve rate cut next month.

Recent data showed that initial jobless claims came in surprisingly low and durable goods orders were stronger than expected, but this did not deter expectations for rate cuts.

Traders are pricing in roughly an 80% probability of a 25 bps cut at the Fed’s final meeting of the year.

Supporting bets on lower rates, President Donald Trump’s key economic adviser, Kevin Hassett, is now seen as the leading contender for Fed chair and is likely to pursue a dovish monetary policy in line with the president’s preferences.

Gold is on track for a fourth consecutive monthly gain, having risen nearly 60% this year and poised for its best annual performance since 1979.

data.title: Kuala Lumpur Kepong's Earnings Outlook May Remain Cautious — Market Talk
data.description: Kuala Lumpur Kepong's earnings outlook may remain cautious, Affin Hwang IB analyst Nadia Aquidah says in a note. She expects crude palm oil prices to stay volatile due to crude palm oil's competitiveness against other vegetable oils and ongoing uncertainties in the agriculture sector and global economy. The analyst cuts KLK's FY 2026-FY 2027 earnings estimates by 5.4%-5.7% to reflect weaker-than-expected contributions from the manufacturing division. Affin Hwang downgrades KLK's rating to hold from buy, and cuts the target price to MYR21.60 from MYR22.20. Shares are 1.5% lower at MYR20.30. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Bank of Japan's Noguchi advocates gradual interest rate hikes
data.description:
Bank of Japan board member Asahi Noguchi said on Thursday the central bank must take a "measured, step-by-step approach" in raising interest rates.

"The BOJ must carefully examine how various economic channels ultimately affect economic activity, prices, and use the policy rate as a tool to adjust the degree of monetary accommodation as appropriate," Noguchi said in a speech.

data.title: Four stocks that should be on your radar due to recent order wins
data.description:
Salasar Techno Engineering has secured contracts worth a combined ₹695.18 crore from Rail Vikas Nigam Ltd.

The scope includes service support and procurement management for electrical and mechanical items, along with erection work to develop distribution infrastructure aimed at reducing losses across the Mandi, Bilaspur, Kullu and Hamirpur circles under the revamped distribution sector scheme.

Indo-Tech Transformers has received a purchase order dated November 25 for the supply of 11 transformers, with the total order value coming in at ₹91.26 crore, excluding taxes.

Patel Engineering has received two Letters of Intent for excavation, overburden removal, coal cutting and related works at the Jhiria West OCP in the Hasdeo area of Bilaspur, Chhattisgarh, for South Eastern Coalfields Ltd. (SECL).

The combined project value stands at ₹798.19 crore, excluding taxes.

The scope covers composite overburden removal including rehandling, coal cutting using surface miners, loading and transportation of coal, along with deployment and maintenance of all required machinery, diesel supply, manpower and labour for seamless execution.

Patel Engineering said its experience in large-scale infrastructure projects positions the company well to deliver the contract efficiently.

"These new projects are a natural extension of the large infrastructure works we undertake and also open fresh avenues for growth. They come on top of a strong tender pipeline of over ₹34,000 crore already bid and under evaluation, and another ₹18,000 crore of opportunities expected before the end of this financial year. Over the next one to two years, nearly ₹1 lakh crore worth of projects are likely to come up for bidding in our core segments. With these opportunities, we are confident of a substantial expansion in our order book," said Managing Director Kavita Shirvaikar.

Brigade Group has signed a 90-year lease deed for 4.859 acres in Technopark Phase I, Thiruvananthapuram, for the development of IT infrastructure.

The land parcel has a development potential of about 1.2 million sq. ft., which will include World Trade Center Trivandrum with A-grade office space and a five-star hotel with over 200 rooms.

data.title: Dollar Extends Fall on Fed Rate Cut Bets
data.description:
The dollar index fell below 99.5 on Thursday, sliding for the fourth straight session to its lowest level in almost two weeks, pressured by growing expectations of further Federal Reserve rate cuts.

Markets now price in roughly an 85% chance of a 25 basis point cut in December, a sharp jump from about 30% a week ago.

Three additional cuts are also being priced in by the end of 2026.

Those expectations strengthened after reports that White House National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett is the leading contender for the next Fed chair, a choice investors view as aligned with President Donald Trump’s preference for lower rates.

Meanwhile, data on Wednesday showed initial jobless claims unexpectedly declined and durable goods orders beat forecasts.

The dollar weakened against most major currencies, posting the largest declines versus the New Zealand and Australian dollar.

Trading volume is expected to stay light through Friday due to the Thanksgiving holiday.

data.title: Kuala Lumpur Kepong Cut to Hold From Buy by Affin Hwang IB >2445.KU
TIME dotCom Shares Gain After Higher Profit, Special Dividend
data.description:
By Ying Xian Wong

TIME dotCom shares gained early Thursday after the company reported an 81% jump in third-quarter profit and declared a special dividend.

Shares of the Malaysian telecommunication company jumped as much as 10.7% and were recently 9.6% higher at 5.23 ringgit, bringing their year-to-date gains to about 18%.

TIME dotCom late Wednesday said its third quarter net profit rose to 106.1 million ringgit, equivalent to $25.7 million, compared with 58.7 million ringgit a year earlier, mainly driven by higher revenue and lower forex losses.

Quarterly revenue was 7.2% higher on year at 453.8 million ringgit.

TIME dotCom also declared a special interim tax-exempt dividend of 0.2164 ringgit a share for the year ending 2025. The 400.1 million ringgit payout will be made on Dec. 22.

CIMB Securities upgraded TIME dotCom's rating to buy from hold, citing the company's improved capital management.

Analyst Choong Chen Foong also sees upside from TIME's plan to optimize its balance sheet, which should lift ROE and support attractive dividend yields over the next two years.

However, Maybank Investment Bank said it expected earnings momentum to ease after 2025, with limited major re-rating catalysts.

Even so, dividend payouts were likely to remain high, and the estimated about 5.5% yield should help support downside risk, said analyst Tan Chi Wei in a note.

Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com

data.title: First Tick: Top global cues to watch in today’s trade
data.description:
Indian benchmark indices, Sensex and Nifty, are likely to see a positive opening on November 27, with cues coming from GIFT Nifty, which was trading higher at around 26,414.50.

Track the latest updates on GIFT Nifty right here on Moneycontrol.

The Indian benchmark indices snapped three-day losing streak, with Nifty index finishing above 26,200 tracking positive global markets amid rising expectations of rate cut by Federal Reserve in its December policy meeting.

The falling crude oil prices on possible Ukraine–Russia peace agreement, also added to the positive sentiments.

At close, the Sensex was up 1,022.50 points or 1.21 percent at 85,609.51, and the Nifty was up 320.50 points or 1.24 percent at 26,205.30. BSE Midcap and small-cap indices rose 1.2% each.

Here is how financial markets across the globe fared overnight:

GIFT Nifty (Gains)

The GIFT Nifty is trading higher at around 26,414.50, indicating a firm opening for the day.

Asian Equities (Gain)

Asian shares followed Wall Street higher as a global equities rally extended, fueled by rising expectations for Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts.

US Equities (Up)

Wall Street extended its rally on Wednesday as revived tech strength, and the increasing probability of a December interest rate cut from the U.S. Federal Reserve put investors in a buying mood the day before the Thanksgiving holiday.

All three major U.S. stock indexes notched their fourth consecutive daily gains, as investors looked past the worries over inflated tech valuations that dragged all three to losses last week.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 314.67 points, or 0.67%, to 47,427.12, the S&P 500 gained 46.73 points, or 0.69%, to 6,812.61, and the Nasdaq Composite gained 189.10 points, or 0.82%, to 23,214.69.

US Bond Yield (Flat)

The yield on 10-year Treasuries and 2-year Treasuries were flat at 3.99 percent and 3.47 percent, respectively.

Dollar Index (Down)

The dollar index has extended its losses to a third day.

Asian Currencies (Gain)

Except Indonesian Rupiah, all other Asian currencies were trading higher in the early trade on Thursday, with the Japanese Yen gain the most.

Crude (Down)

Oil edged lower as investors tracked US-led efforts to end the war in Ukraine, while looking

ahead to an OPEC+ gathering this weekend.

Gold (Flat)

Gold steadied, having gained nearly 1% in the previous session on growing expectations of another

interest-rate cut in the US.

Fund Flow Action

The Foreign Institutional Investors (FIIs) continued their buying on second day as they bought equities of Rs 4778 crore on November 26, while Domestic Institutional Investors (DIIs) bought equities of Rs 6247 crore on the same day.
